section.title: Deliver Me from Evil Men
section.sectionRationale: A Davidic lament against violent, deceitful enemies, this psalm belongs to the cluster of individual laments, Psalms 140-143, that close out Book V before David's final blessing psalms, its confidence that the LORD upholds justice for the poor and needy grounding honest prayer against real, documented cruelty in trust rather than personal revenge.
section.thesis: Surrounded by violent men whose tongues are as sharp as serpents and whose traps are hidden along his path, David refuses to take justice into his own hands, instead crying out to the LORD as his shield in the day of battle and resting in the certainty that God upholds the cause of the afflicted and needy.
section.frameNote: Four movements marked by Selah: the cry for rescue from violent, deceitful men, verses 1-3; the plea for protection from hidden traps, verses 4-5; confident petition to God the deliverer, verses 6-8; and trust in God's justice for evildoers and for the needy, verses 9-13.

units.label: Rescue me from evil men
units.note: the cry for rescue from violent, deceitful men

verses.text: Rescue me, O LORD, from evil men. Protect me from men of violence,
who devise evil in their hearts and stir up war all day long.
They sharpen their tongues like snakes; the venom of vipers is on their lips. Selah

units.label: Protection from hidden traps
units.note: the plea against snares and nets

verses.text: Guard me, O LORD, from the hands of the wicked. Keep me safe from men of violence who scheme to make me stumble.
The proud hide a {{pach|snare}} for me; the cords of their net are spread along the path, and lures are set out for me. Selah
I say to the LORD, "You are my God." Hear, O LORD, my cry for help.
O GOD the Lord, the strength of my salvation, You shield my head in the day of battle.
Grant not, O LORD, the desires of the wicked; do not promote their evil plans, lest they be exalted. Selah
May the heads of those who surround me be covered in the trouble their lips have caused.
May burning coals fall on them; may they be thrown into the fire, into the miry pits, never to rise again.
May no slanderer be established in the land; may calamity hunt down the man of violence.
I know that the LORD upholds {{mishpat|justice}} for the poor and defends the cause of the needy.
Surely the righteous will praise Your name; the upright will dwell in Your presence.

pach.gloss: A snare, trap, or net, the sort hunters spread along a game trail. David pictures the proud hiding such traps along his own path, language that reappears almost identically at the close of Psalm 141, showing the same danger pressing David through this cluster of laments.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, the legal right due to someone. David's confidence rests not in his own strength but in his certainty that the LORD Himself upholds mishpat for the poor and needy, the ground of every prayer in this psalm for deliverance and vindication.

basic.context: David cries for rescue from violent men whose words are as deadly as a serpent's venom and who set hidden traps along his path, likely reflecting seasons such as Saul's pursuit or Absalom's rebellion when slander and ambush were constant threats.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes a real, historical danger David faced and the imprecatory prayers he offered in response; it prescribes bringing violent, deceitful opposition honestly to God as refuge and judge rather than taking retribution into one's own hands.
basic.originalLanguages: The proud hide a pach, snare, along David's path, the same trap imagery repeated at the close of Psalm 141. David's confidence rests on his certainty that the LORD upholds mishpat, justice, for the poor and needy, the legal vindication he cannot secure for himself.
basic.typology: David's plea, You shield my head in the day of battle, verse 7, anticipates the full armor of God believers are called to put on for spiritual warfare, Ephesians 6:10-17, and his confidence that God upholds the afflicted anticipates Christ's own care for the poor and oppressed, Luke 4:18.
basic.christ: Paul quotes verse 3's description of venomous speech in Romans 3:13 to describe universal human sinfulness, showing that the violence and deceit David faced from without also lodges within every human heart, a condition only Christ's righteousness can address, Romans 3:21-26.
basic.god: God is shown as shield, deliverer, and righteous judge, one who guards the vulnerable from hidden traps, restrains the schemes of the proud, and can be trusted to uphold justice for the poor and needy even when human courts fail.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3:13 quotes verse 3 to describe the deceitful, venomous speech common to fallen humanity. Ephesians 6:10-17 develops the psalm's shield and battle imagery into the full armor of God for spiritual warfare. Romans 12:19 forbids personal revenge and entrusts justice to God, the same posture David models here.
basic.oneStory: David's cry for rescue from violent, deceitful enemies and his confidence that God upholds justice for the poor and needy anticipate the New Testament's diagnosis of universal sin and its promise of a Deliverer who shields His people in every spiritual battle and will finally right every wrong.

context.literary: An individual lament in four movements marked by Selah, moving from cry, verses 1-3, to plea for protection, verses 4-5, to confident petition, verses 6-8, to trust in God's justice for the wicked and vindication for the righteous, verses 9-13.
context.historical: Attributed to David, the language of violent men, slander, and hidden traps fits seasons such as Saul's pursuit, 1 Samuel 23-24, or Absalom's rebellion, 2 Samuel 15, both marked by treachery from within David's own circles.
context.canonical: The first of a cluster of Davidic laments, Psalms 140-143, that close Book V before turning toward the final blessing and praise psalms, 144-150, giving voice to honest distress even near the end of the Psalter's arc.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To cry out honestly for rescue from violent, deceitful enemies, to ask God to prevent their schemes from succeeding, and to ground continued trust in God's established justice for the poor and needy rather than in personal retaliation.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific historical danger and his imprecatory requests against particular enemies; it prescribes the abiding pattern of taking violent opposition to God in prayer and trusting His justice rather than seeking private vengeance.

debates.question: How should the imprecations of verses 9-10, burning coals, fire, and miry pits never to rise again, be understood?

views.label: As prayer that evildoers experience the natural consequences of the trouble their own words have caused, a form of poetic justice entrusted to God's hand
views.case: Verse 9 specifically asks that their heads be covered in the trouble their own lips have caused, suggesting the punishment fits and flows from their own scheming rather than being an arbitrary escalation.

views.label: As urgent, honest prayer for God's decisive judgment on persistent, violent evil, using vivid ancient Near Eastern imagery of siege warfare and defeat
views.case: The imagery of burning coals and miry pits reflects real ancient battle conditions, and the psalm's urgency reflects a genuine desire that God act decisively and soon against ongoing violent oppression, not merely poetic symmetry.

debates.bearing: Both readings keep the prayer directed at God rather than at personal action, and both are answered ultimately in God's own righteous judgment, whether through measured consequence or decisive intervention, never through David's own hand.

study.originalLanguages: The proud hide a pach, snare, along David's path, echoing the same trap imagery closing Psalm 141. His confidence rests on the LORD upholding mishpat, justice, for the poor and needy, the vindication David cannot secure for himself but trusts God to provide.

typology.type: You shield my head in the day of battle, verse 7
typology.fulfillment: The full armor of God given to believers for spiritual warfare, Ephesians 6:10-17.

typology.type: The LORD upholding justice for the poor and needy, verse 12
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own mission to bring good news and justice to the poor and oppressed, Luke 4:18.

study.christ: Paul quotes verse 3's description of venomous, deceitful speech in Romans 3:13 as part of his indictment of universal human sinfulness, showing that the violence David faced from without reflects a condition within every heart, addressed only by the righteousness God provides in Christ, Romans 3:21-26.
study.god: God is shield, deliverer, and righteous judge, guarding the vulnerable from hidden traps, restraining the schemes of the proud, and upholding justice for the poor and needy even when no human court will.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes this psalm's description of venomous, deceitful speech, verse 3, as evidence of universal human sinfulness.
Develops the psalm's shield and battle imagery, verse 7, into the full armor of God for spiritual warfare.
Forbids personal vengeance and entrusts justice to God, the posture David models throughout this psalm.

study.oneStory: David's cry for rescue from violent, deceitful enemies and his trust that God upholds justice for the poor and needy anticipate both the New Testament's diagnosis of universal sin and its promise of a Deliverer who shields His people in every battle and will finally right every wrong.

quiz.q: How does David describe the words of the violent men opposing him in verse 3?

options.text: Their tongues are sharpened like snakes, with the venom of vipers on their lips.
options.note: This vivid image of deadly, deceitful speech is quoted by Paul in Romans 3:13.

options.text: Their words are harmless but their actions are dangerous.
options.note: The verse specifically targets their speech itself as venomous and deadly, not merely their actions.

options.text: Their words are confused and lack any real threat.
options.note: The imagery is of sharp, deliberate, deadly speech, not confusion.

options.text: Their words are honest but harsh in tone.
options.note: The venom imagery signals deceitful, destructive speech, not simply blunt honesty.

quiz.q: What have the proud done according to verse 5?

options.text: Hidden a snare and spread cords of a net along the path David walks, with lures set out for him.
options.note: This hunting imagery pictures deliberate, hidden schemes designed to trap David unaware.

options.text: Openly declared war on David in a public battle.
options.note: The imagery is of hidden, secret traps, not open, declared warfare.

options.text: Offered David a peace treaty he refused to sign.
options.note: The verse describes concealed danger, not any offer of peace.

options.text: Built a wall to keep David out of the city.
options.note: The image is of snares and nets along a path, not a defensive wall.

quiz.q: What confession does David make in verse 6?

options.text: "You are my God," a direct declaration of trust and allegiance to the LORD amid danger.
options.note: This simple, personal confession anchors the psalm's confident petition that follows.

options.text: That he no longer trusts God to help him.
options.note: The confession is one of trust, the opposite of despair or doubt.

options.text: That he will handle the situation using his own military strength alone.
options.note: The verse turns to God for help rather than relying on self-sufficiency.

options.text: That his enemies are actually more powerful than God.
options.note: The confession affirms God's supremacy and calls on Him as deliverer, not the reverse.

quiz.q: What does David know for certain about the LORD, according to verse 12?

options.text: That the LORD upholds justice for the poor and defends the cause of the needy.
options.note: This settled confidence in God's justice grounds the whole psalm's trust rather than despair.

options.text: That the LORD only helps kings and never the poor.
options.note: The verse specifically affirms God's care for the poor and needy, not only the powerful.

options.text: That justice in this life is impossible to find.
options.note: The verse expresses confident certainty that God does uphold justice, not hopelessness about it.

options.text: That David himself must personally punish every wrongdoer.
options.note: The verse credits the LORD, not David's own hand, with upholding justice.

quiz.q: What does David expect for the righteous, according to verse 13?

options.text: That the righteous will praise God's name and the upright will dwell in His presence.
options.note: This closing confidence turns the lament toward worship and future fellowship with God.

options.text: That the righteous will suffer forever with no relief.
options.note: The verse expects the opposite, praise and dwelling in God's presence for the upright.

options.text: That the righteous will eventually become just as violent as their enemies.
options.note: The psalm contrasts the righteous with violent men throughout, not equates them.

options.text: That only David alone, and no one else, will be vindicated.
options.note: The verse speaks broadly of the righteous and the upright, not David exclusively.

questions: David names his enemies' venomous speech directly to God rather than retaliating in kind. What deceitful or hurtful words are you facing right now, and what would it look like to bring them honestly to God instead of responding in kind?
Hidden snares were set along David's own path. Where do you sense hidden temptation or danger along your path this week, and how can you ask God to guard you from it?
David's confession, You are my God, anchors his whole prayer. What would change in your current situation if you began there, simply reaffirming whose you are, before asking for anything else?
David entrusts judgment of his enemies to God rather than acting himself. Is there a situation where you are tempted to take justice into your own hands rather than trusting God's timing?

David is certain the LORD upholds justice for the poor and needy even when he cannot see it happening yet. Where do you need that same certainty today, in a situation where justice still seems delayed?

translationNotes.note: Paul quotes this verse directly in Romans 3:13 as part of his catalog of Old Testament texts proving universal human sinfulness, the venom of asps under their lips.

May the heads of those who surround me be covered in the trouble their lips have caused reflects a common psalm pattern in which the evildoer's own scheme becomes the very trouble that falls back upon them.
Justice renders the Hebrew mishpat, a legal term for the right verdict or vindication due to someone, here confidently attributed to the LORD's ongoing care for the poor and needy.

meta.notes: BSB text retrieved via WebSearch and web_fetch from biblehub.com (local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error). No em or en dashes were present in the source text for this psalm. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg/truthforlife.org, Keller/gospelinlife.com, Piper/desiringgod.org, Ferguson, Carson/thegospelcoalition.org) specifically on Psalm 140 was found via WebSearch this session, so sermon status is recorded as none rather than fabricated.
